revision.text:
The '''Kumanotaisha Shrine''' (熊野大社, ''kumanotaisha'') is an historic [[shinto]] shrine in [[Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]], [[Nanyo]], [[Yamagata]], [[Japan]].
It dates from the 9th century, and is one of only three large Kumano shrines in Japan.

The '''Kumanotaisha Shrine''' (熊野大社, ''kumanotaisha'') is an historic [[shinto]] shrine in [[Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]], [[Nanyo]], [[Yamagata]], [[Japan]].
It dates from the 9th century, and is one of only three large Kumano shrines in Japan.
[[Category:Shinto shrines]]

revision.comment: moved [[Kumanotaisha Shrine]] to [[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]]: There is a same name shrine in Shimane

{{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} is  a historic [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the [[:en:Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nan'yō, Yamagata|Nan'yō, Yamagata]], in the [[:en:Tohoku region|Tohoku region]] of northern Japan. It is commonly referred to as the "Kumano Taisha", although the shrine does not officially have a "Taisha" designation. The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.

==History==
The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]] by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].  In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]], [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}

The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

==External links==
* {{Official website|http://www.kumano-taisha.or.jp}} {{in lang|ja}}

The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.

==History==
The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]] by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].  In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]], [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}

The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]] by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].  In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]], [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}

The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.

The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.

==History==
The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]] by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].  In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]], [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}

The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} is  a historic [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the [[:en:Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nan'yō, Yamagata|Nan'yō, Yamagata]], in the [[:en:Tohoku region|Tohoku region]] of northern Japan. It is commonly referred to as the "Kumano Taisha", although the shrine does not officially have a "Taisha" designation. The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.

==History==
The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]] by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].  In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]], [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.

Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|tsurigane]]''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}

The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei}} as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province}}. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} by {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori}} by order of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}. In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan}}, {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.
Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=tsurigane}}''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}
The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}{{More citations needed|date=February 2024}}{{infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} | image = File:YamagatakenNanyoushi-KumanoTaisha-01.jpg | caption = Kumano Shrine | map_type = Japan Yamagata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.07805556|N|140.13694444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | type = [[Kumano shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = c.806 AD | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nan'yō, Yamagata|en|Nan'yō, Yamagata|WD=Q850496|lt=Nan'yō, Yamagata}}, Japan | website = {{official|1=http://www.kumano-taisha.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = July 25 | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Miyauchi|WD=Q3317302}}; {{ill|Nanyo|WD=Q850496}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} is a historic {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the [[:en:Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]] neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nan'yō, Yamagata|en|Nan'yō, Yamagata|WD=Q850496|lt=Nan'yō, Yamagata}}, in the [[:en:Tohoku region|Tohoku region]] of northern Japan. It is commonly referred to as the "Kumano Taisha", although the shrine does not officially have a "Taisha" designation. The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.
==History== The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei}} as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province}}. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} by {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori}} by order of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}. In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan}}, {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.
Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=tsurigane}}''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}
The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح كومانو]][[co:commons:Category:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[en:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[fr:Kumano-taisha (Yamagata)]][[it:Santuario kumano di Yamagata]][[ja:熊野神社 (南陽市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح كومانو]][[co:commons:Category:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[en:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[fr:Kumano-taisha (Yamagata)]][[it:Santuario kumano di Yamagata]][[ja:熊野神社 (南陽市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح كومانو]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[en:Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]][[fr:Kumano-taisha (Yamagata)]][[it:Santuario kumano di Yamagata]][[ja:熊野神社 (南陽市)]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}{{More citations needed|date=February 2024}}{{infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} | image = File:YamagatakenNanyoushi-KumanoTaisha-01.jpg | caption = Kumano Shrine | map_type = Japan Yamagata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.07805556|N|140.13694444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} | type = [[Kumano shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = c.806 AD | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nan'yō, Yamagata|en|Nan'yō, Yamagata|WD=Q850496|lt=Nan'yō, Yamagata|1=Nan'yō, Yamagata}}, Japan | website = {{official|1=http://www.kumano-taisha.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = July 25 | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Miyauchi|WD=Q3317302|1=Miyauchi, Yamagata}}; {{ill|Nanyo|WD=Q850496|1=Nan'yō, Yamagata}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|Kumano-jinja}} is a historic {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the [[:en:Miyauchi, Nanyo|Miyauchi]] neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nan'yō, Yamagata|en|Nan'yō, Yamagata|WD=Q850496|lt=Nan'yō, Yamagata|1=Nan'yō, Yamagata}}, in the [[:en:Tohoku region|Tohoku region]] of northern Japan. It is commonly referred to as the "Kumano Taisha", although the shrine does not officially have a "Taisha" designation. The shrine is one of the three main centers of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano cult]] within Shinto.
==History== The origins of the shrine are uncertain. The shrine claims to have been founded in 806 AD by {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei|1=Emperor Heizei}} as a branch of the famous [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, and the shrine was associated with the [[:en:Kokubun-ji|provincial temple]] of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}}. However, an alternative history states that the shrine was built in the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} by {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}} by order of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}. In either case, the shrine was patronized by the successive feudal lords of the region, including the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan|1=Date clan}}, {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} and [[Uesugi clan]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] and the creation of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|shrine was Ranked]] as a Prefectural Shrine.
Two main features of the shrine are a giant tree which locals believe to be several hundred years old, and a traditional {{Nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=tsurigane|1=Bonshō}}''|釣り鐘}} bell. The bell is unique because during World War II, most large iron bells in Japan were melted down for their material. The bell at the shrine was saved by order of the Emperor, and is thus one of the few original preserved bells in Japan.{{Citation needed|date=March 2013}}
The Kumano-Taisha festival is held at the shrine each year on July 25.<ref>{{Cite web |title=熊野大社例大祭 |url=http://www.city.nanyo.yamagata.jp/kankoibento/618 |website=Nanyo City |language=ja}}</ref>
